common_misuse: Some businesses assume CCPA only handles individual complaints. In reality, CCPA can initiate class-action-style investigations affecting entire industries.
judicial_interpretation: CCPA has actively used its powers to issue guidelines on dark patterns (2023) and take action against misleading e-commerce practices.

title: Jurisdiction of District Commission
full_text: Subject to the other provisions of this Act, the District Commission shall have jurisdiction to entertain complaints where the value of the goods or services paid as consideration does not exceed one crore rupees.
plain_language_summary: The District Consumer Commission handles complaints for goods/services worth up to ₹1 crore. This is your local, accessible forum for resolving consumer disputes without needing expensive lawyers.
practical_implication: Most consumer complaints (products, subscriptions, services, small loans) fall within this ₹1 crore limit. Filing is simple, can be done online via edaakhil.nic.in, and lawyer representation is optional.
common_misuse: Companies include clauses like 'exclusive jurisdiction of courts in [their city]' to discourage consumer complaints. Consumer Forum jurisdiction is based on where the consumer resides or where the cause of action arose — not the company's location.
judicial_interpretation: Consumer Forums have consistently rejected corporate attempts to restrict jurisdiction through contract clauses. The consumer can file where they live.

title: Relief available to consumers — orders by District Commission
full_text: The District Commission may order: removal of defects, replacement of goods, refund of price, payment of compensation for loss or injury, discontinuance of unfair/restrictive trade practices, adequate costs to parties, and such other relief as it deems fit.
plain_language_summary: The Consumer Forum can order a business to fix the product, replace it, refund your money, pay you compensation for your trouble, and even pay your legal costs. These are powerful remedies available to every consumer.
practical_implication: Consumer Forums have wide discretion in providing relief. They can order compensation for mental agony, loss of time, and inconvenience — not just the product value. They can also impose punitive damages for willful deficiency.
common_misuse: Companies often try to limit remedies to 'replacement only' or 'refund only' in their contracts. Consumer Forums are not bound by these contractual limitations.
judicial_interpretation: Courts have awarded compensation well above the product value for mental agony, inconvenience, and loss of time — especially when the company was willfully deficient.

title: Jurisdiction of Consumer Commission — territorial
full_text: A complaint shall be instituted in the Consumer Forum within the local limits of whose jurisdiction the opposite party resides or carries on business, or where the cause of action wholly or partly arises.
plain_language_summary: You can file your consumer complaint in the city where YOU live or where the business operates — whichever is more convenient for you. This prevents companies from forcing you to travel to their headquarters city.
practical_implication: This is one of the most consumer-friendly provisions. Even if a company's TOS says 'exclusive jurisdiction of Delhi courts,' you can file in your local Consumer Forum. The contract clause cannot override this statutory right.
common_misuse: Companies routinely include 'exclusive jurisdiction of [company HQ] courts' clauses to discourage litigation. These are void against Consumer Forum jurisdiction.
judicial_interpretation: The Supreme Court has repeatedly held that contractual jurisdiction clauses cannot oust the statutory jurisdiction of Consumer Forums.

title: Product liability — manufacturer, seller, and service provider
full_text: A product liability action may be brought by any person against a product manufacturer, product seller, or product service provider for any harm caused by a defective product manufactured, sold, or serviced by them. The liability extends to design defects, manufacturing defects, deviation from specifications, failure to warn, and non-conformance to express warranty.
plain_language_summary: If a product causes you harm — whether through a design flaw, manufacturing defect, or failure to include warnings — you can sue the manufacturer, seller, AND service provider. All three can be held liable.
practical_implication: This creates a chain of liability. You don't need to prove which specific party was negligent — if the product was defective and caused harm, all parties in the chain are liable. This is especially powerful against e-commerce platforms.
common_misuse: Sellers try to disclaim product liability ('as-is, no warranty'). Product liability under CPA 2019 is a statutory right that cannot be contractually waived.
judicial_interpretation: Product liability provisions are relatively new (2019) but courts are applying them broadly, especially for online purchases where the manufacturer may be overseas.

title: Registration of real estate projects
full_text: No promoter shall advertise, market, book, sell or offer for sale, or invite persons to purchase in any manner any plot, apartment or building in any real estate project without registering the real estate project with the Real Estate Regulatory Authority.
plain_language_summary: Builders cannot sell, advertise, or even accept bookings for any real estate project without first registering it with RERA. This gives buyers access to verified project details, timelines, and financial information.
practical_implication: Always check if a project is RERA-registered before buying. RERA registration means the builder has disclosed project plans, financial details, completion timelines, and is under regulatory oversight.
common_misuse: Some builders sell 'pre-launch' units before RERA registration to avoid regulatory scrutiny. This is illegal. Also, some projects are deliberately kept below the registration threshold (500 sqm or 8 units).
judicial_interpretation: RERA authorities have imposed heavy penalties on builders for selling without registration, including refund of the entire amount with interest.

title: Application for registration — disclosures by promoter
full_text: The promoter shall disclose: sanctioned plans, approved layout, project timeline, type of fixtures and specifications, details of structural engineer and architect, and information related to the carpet area, exclusive balcony, or verandah area.
plain_language_summary: Builders must disclose everything about the project upfront — approved plans, exact carpet area (not super built-up area), materials and specifications, and the timeline for completion. No more vague promises.
practical_implication: Before RERA, builders routinely quoted 'super built-up area' (inflated by 30-40%). Now they must disclose 'carpet area' — what you actually get. They must also deposit 70% of buyer payments in a separate escrow account.
common_misuse: Some builders disclose minimum specifications and later downgrade materials. RERA requires adherence to disclosed specifications. Any deviation requires buyer consent.
judicial_interpretation: RERA authorities have ordered refunds with interest when builders deviated from disclosed specifications or delayed beyond the registered timeline.

title: Return of amount and compensation — delayed possession
full_text: If the promoter fails to complete or is unable to give possession of an apartment, plot or building, he shall be liable on demand to the allottees to return the amount received by him in respect of that apartment, plot, building, with interest at such rate as may be prescribed.
plain_language_summary: If the builder delays possession beyond the promised date, you have two choices: (1) Walk away and get a FULL REFUND with interest, or (2) Stay and get compensation for every month of delay. The interest rate is set by RERA (typically SBI MCLR + 2%).
practical_implication: This is RERA's most powerful provision. Before RERA, buyers waited years with no remedy. Now, delayed possession triggers automatic refund rights with interest at rates far higher than typical savings accounts.
common_misuse: Builders include 'grace period' clauses (6-12 months extra) and 'force majeure' expansions (including 'government approvals') to extend deadlines. RERA authorities scrutinize these heavily.
judicial_interpretation: The Supreme Court in Newtech Promoters v. State of UP (2021) held that RERA refund rights cannot be waived by contract and builder-buyer agreements cannot override RERA protections.

act_name: RBI Fair Practices Code for Lenders
title: Fair Practices Code for NBFCs and Banks
full_text: The lender shall provide the borrower with: all-inclusive interest rate, processing fee, prepayment charges, and other charges on the loan in a standardized Key Fact Statement. The borrower shall be given adequate time to compare loan offers. The lender shall not interfere with the borrower's right to prepay. Penal charges on loan defaults must be reasonable and not used as a revenue-enhancing tool.
plain_language_summary: Banks and NBFCs must give you a clear 'Key Fact Statement' showing the real cost of your loan — interest rate, all fees, and total repayment amount. They cannot hide charges, refuse prepayment, or impose excessive penalties for late payment.
practical_implication: Always ask for the Key Fact Statement (KFS) before signing any loan. It shows the Annual Percentage Rate (APR), which is the true cost including all fees. Compare APR across lenders, not just the headline interest rate.
common_misuse: Some NBFCs and fintech lenders advertise low interest rates but charge high processing fees, insurance premiums, and documentation charges that dramatically increase the effective cost.
judicial_interpretation: RBI has taken action against lenders who violate the Fair Practices Code, including fines and license restrictions. Banking Ombudsman complaints are effective for individual borrowers.

title: Prohibition on prepayment penalty for floating rate home loans
full_text: Banks are not permitted to charge foreclosure charges / pre-payment penalties on home loans on floating interest rate basis, with immediate effect. This instruction is applicable to all existing as well as new home loan borrowers.
plain_language_summary: If you have a home loan on a floating interest rate, the bank CANNOT charge you ANY penalty for prepaying or foreclosing the loan — whether you pay part of it early or close the entire loan. This applies to all existing and new loans.
practical_implication: This is one of the most borrower-friendly RBI regulations. If your bank charges prepayment fees on a floating-rate home loan, they are violating RBI guidelines. File a complaint with the Banking Ombudsman.
common_misuse: Some banks still include prepayment penalty clauses in their standard loan agreements for floating-rate home loans, hoping borrowers won't know about this circular. Others apply hidden 'administrative charges' instead of calling it a prepayment penalty.
judicial_interpretation: Banking Ombudsman has consistently ruled against banks charging prepayment penalties on floating-rate home loans and ordered refunds.

act_name: CCPA Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns, 2023
title: Prohibition of dark patterns in e-commerce and digital platforms
full_text: No person, including any platform, shall engage in any dark pattern. Dark patterns include: false urgency, basket sneaking, confirm shaming, forced action, subscription trap, interface interference, bait and switch, hidden costs, disguised advertisement, nagging, trick questions, SaaS billing, and rogue malwares.
plain_language_summary: E-commerce platforms and digital services are banned from using design tricks ('dark patterns') that manipulate you into making purchases, subscribing, or sharing data against your will. This includes fake countdown timers, hidden charges added at checkout, making cancellation difficult, and subscription traps.
practical_implication: If a website uses fake 'only 2 left!' urgency, sneaks insurance into your cart, makes the 'subscribe' button bright green but the 'no thanks' link tiny and gray, or requires 15 steps to cancel a subscription — that's a dark pattern and it's illegal.
common_misuse: Many major platforms continue to use dark patterns despite the guidelines. Document the dark pattern (screenshot) and file a complaint with CCPA via consumerhelpline.gov.in.
judicial_interpretation: CCPA has begun taking action under these guidelines, particularly against e-commerce platforms for basket sneaking (adding products/insurance to cart without consent) and subscription traps.

title: Implied conditions as to quality or fitness
full_text: Where the buyer, expressly or by implication, makes known to the seller the particular purpose for which the goods are required, so as to show that the buyer relies on the seller's skill or judgment, and the goods are of a description which it is in the course of the seller's business to supply, there is an implied condition that the goods shall be reasonably fit for such purpose.
plain_language_summary: When you buy something for a specific purpose and you rely on the seller's expertise, the law implies a warranty that the product will be fit for that purpose — even if the seller doesn't explicitly promise it. This applies to all sales, not just consumer purchases.
practical_implication: This is the legal basis for challenging 'AS IS / NO WARRANTY' disclaimers. If you tell a SaaS vendor you need software for invoicing and they sell you their product, there's an implied warranty that it actually works for invoicing — even if their contract says 'no warranties.'
common_misuse: Vendors routinely disclaim 'all warranties express or implied.' However, implied conditions under Section 16 cannot be entirely excluded when the buyer relied on the seller's skill and judgment.
judicial_interpretation: Courts have applied Section 16 to hold sellers liable even when contracts contained warranty disclaimers, particularly where the seller was aware of the buyer's specific needs.
